perturbation.original_text: Licensee agrees that the business operated by it in connection with the Licensed Mark shall comply with all laws, rules, regulations and requirements of any governmental body in the Territory or elsewhere as may be applicable to the operation, marketing, and promotion of the business and shall notify Licensor of any action that must be taken by Licensee to comply with such law, rules, regulations or requirements.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee agrees that the business operated by it in connection with the Licensed Mark shall use reasonable efforts to comply with all laws, rules, regulations and requirements of any governmental body in the Territory or elsewhere as may be applicable to the operation, marketing, and promotion of the business and shall notify Licensor of any action that must be taken by Licensee to comply with such law, rules, regulations or requirements.
perturbation.explanation: By adding "reasonable efforts," the licensee is no longer strictly obligated to comply with all applicable laws. This weakens the compliance requirement, potentially creating a loophole where non-compliance could be justified. Given that Palmer Square Capital BDC Inc. is registered in Maryland and operating in the financial sector, strict adherence to Maryland's financial regulations is crucial, and this modification introduces legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Annotated Code, Corporations and Associations, § 1-402
perturbation.law_explanation: MD Code, Corporations and Associations, § 1-402 requires corporations operating in Maryland to conduct business in a manner that complies with all applicable laws and regulations. The introduction of "reasonable efforts" weakens the obligation to comply with these laws, potentially creating a legal contradiction if the licensee's 'reasonable efforts' fall short of actual compliance, putting the corporation at legal risk.

A determination required or permitted to be made under any provision of this article relating to stated capital, surplus, capital surplus, earned surplus, or any other account or matter relating to the financial position or results of operations of a Maryland corporation is prima facie proper and in accordance with this article if: (1) Specific provisions of this article do not require otherwise; and (2) It is made in good faith in accordance with generally accepted accounting practices and principles. Previous Next Validation Please fix the following: OK Click to return on the top page
perturbation.justification: The original text requires strict compliance with all applicable laws, rules, and regulations. The changed text weakens this obligation by requiring only 'reasonable efforts' to comply. This introduces a legal contradiction because, according to the Law Explanation and MD Code, Corporations and Associations, § 1-402, corporations operating in Maryland are generally expected to conduct business in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations. The 'reasonable efforts' standard could potentially fall short of actual compliance, creating a contradiction with the expectation of full compliance under Maryland law.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that Investment Advisor shall be a third party beneficiary of this Agreement, and shall have the rights and protections provided to Licensee under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that Investment Advisor may be considered a third party beneficiary of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be' to 'may be considered' introduces ambiguity regarding the rights and protections afforded to Investment Advisor as a third-party beneficiary. Under Maryland law, specifically regarding contract law and third-party beneficiaries, clear intent is needed. This introduces legal risk as to whether Investment Advisor can enforce provisions of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Contract Law regarding Third-Party Beneficiaries
perturbation.law_citation: Spates v. Spates, 91 Md. App. 655, 605 A.2d 990 (1992)
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law requires a clear and definite intent to benefit a third party for that party to be considered a third-party beneficiary with enforceable rights. By changing the language to 'may be considered,' the agreement introduces uncertainty about whether the Investment Advisor is truly intended to benefit from the contract, which would undermine the enforceability of any rights or protections they might otherwise have.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be' to 'may be considered' significantly weakens the intended beneficiary status of the Investment Advisor. This introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of rights previously guaranteed under the original agreement. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that Maryland law requires a clear and definite intent to benefit a third party. The modified language introduces uncertainty, creating a legal contradiction because it undermines the legally mandated clarity required for third-party beneficiary status. Since both law snippets are unavailable, I am relying on the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation to assess the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York. The parties unconditionally and irrevocably consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of New York and waive any objection with respect thereto, for the purpose of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated hereby.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause where the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts located in the State of New York removes a clear agreement on venue, which introduces ambiguity regarding where legal disputes would be resolved. Since Palmer Square Capital BDC Inc. is incorporated in Maryland, and the contract involves operations potentially impacting Maryland, removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause allows for legal challenges to be brought in Maryland, which could create legal complications, added expenses, and potential conflicts of laws between Maryland and New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103
perturbation.law_citation: MD Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103
perturbation.law_explanation: MD Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103 addresses the jurisdiction of Maryland courts. By removing the clause consenting to New York jurisdiction, the contract implicitly opens the door to litigation in Maryland courts, potentially conflicting with the agreement's intent to operate under New York law. This can increase litigation costs and create uncertainty, especially if the operations have significant ties to Maryland.

(a) If jurisdiction over a person is based solely upon this section, he may be sued only on a cause of action arising from any act enumerated in this section. (b) A court may exercise personal jurisdiction over a person, who directly or by an agent: (1) Transacts any business or performs any character of work or service in the State; (2) Contracts to supply goods, food, services, or manufactured products in the State; (3) Causes tortious injury in the State by an act or omission in the State; (4) Causes tortious injury in the State or outside of the State by an act or omission outside the State if he regularly does or solicits business, engages in any other persistent course of conduct in the State or derives substantial revenue from goods, food, services, or manufactured products used or consumed in the State; (5) Has an interest in, uses, or possesses real property in the State; or (6) Contracts to insure or act as surety for, or on, any person, property, risk, contract, obligation, or agreement located, executed, or to be performed within the State at the time the contract is made, unless the parties otherwise provide in writing. (c) (1) (i) In this subsection the following terms have the meanings indicated. (ii) “Computer information” has the meaning stated in § 22-102 of the Commercial Law Article. (iii) “Computer program” has the meaning stated in § 22-102 of the Commercial Law Article. (2) The provisions of this section apply to computer information and computer programs in the same manner as they apply to goods and services. Previous Next Validation Please fix the following: OK Click to return on the top page
perturbation.justification: The original text specified exclusive jurisdiction in New York, but the changed text omits this clause, opening the door to potential litigation in other jurisdictions like Maryland, as the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest. MD Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 6-103 (b)(1) states that a Maryland court may exercise personal jurisdiction over a person who transacts any business or performs any character of work or service in the State. Therefore, removing the New York jurisdiction clause directly contradicts the intent of the original contract and introduces a legal risk related to jurisdiction, creating a legal contradiction by omission.
